17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.

42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.

108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.

148	These run automatically between phases:

117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
130	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
131	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
132	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
133	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
134	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
135	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
136	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
137	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
138	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
139	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
140	
141	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
142	
143	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
144	
145	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
146	
147	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
148	
149	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
150	
151	## Always read everything
152	
153	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
154	
155	## Unstarted-phase proposals
156	
157	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
158	
159	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. For each phase row whose `Status` is not `complete` and whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
160	
161	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
162	
163	The proposal answers:
164	
165	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
166	
167	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
168	
169	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
170	
171	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
172	
173	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
174	
175	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
176	
177	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
178	
179	```

181	
182	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
183	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
184	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
185	
186	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
187	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
188	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
189	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
190	...
191	
192	**`\uses` skeleton**:
193	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
194	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
195	
196	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
197	
198	**References for writer**:
199	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
200	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
201	
202	**Subphase choices exposed**:
203	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
204	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
205	```
206	
207	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
208	
209	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.

211	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.

7	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
8	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
9	
10	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
11	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
12	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
13	\[
14	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
15	  \qquad
16	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
17	\]
18	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
19	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
20	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
21	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
22	
23	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
24	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
25	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
26	% groups)
27	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
28	% SOURCE QUOTE:
29	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
30	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
31	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
32	% abelian groups defined by the formula
33	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
34	% \end{dfn}
35	%  [\dots]
36	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
37	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
39	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
40	% topologies  by
41	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
42	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
43	%  \end{dfn}"
44	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
45	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
46	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
47	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
48	homomorphisms.}
49	
50	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
51	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
52	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
53	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
54	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
55	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
56	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
57	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
58	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
59	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
60	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
61	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
62	structure. This is the content of
63	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
64	
65	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
66	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
67	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
68	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
69	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
70	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
71	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
72	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
73	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
74	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
75	\begin{enumerate}
76	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
77	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
78	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
79	    isomorphism classes;
80	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
81	    for \(\otimes\);
82	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
83	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
84	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
85	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
86	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
87	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
88	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
89	\end{enumerate}
90	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
91	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
92	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
93	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
94	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
95	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
96	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
97	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
98	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
99	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
100	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
101	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
102	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
103	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
104	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
105	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
106	supplied.
107	
108	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
109	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
110	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
111	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
112	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
113	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
114	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
115	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
116	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
117	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
118	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
119	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
120	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
121	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
122	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
123	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
124	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
125	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
126	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
127	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
128	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
129	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
130	avoids. The
131	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
132	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
133	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
134	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
135	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
136	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
137	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
138	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
139	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
140

5	\section{The equivalence}
6	\label{sec:soe_equivalence}
7	
8	\begin{definition}
9	
10	\leanok
11	[Sheaf-of-modules over-equivalence]
12	  \label{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overEquivalence}
14	  % SOURCE: Mathlib code-location provenance (this is an Archon-original assembly
15	  % of existing Mathlib primitives, NOT an external literature citation, so it
16	  % carries no verbatim mathematical SOURCE QUOTE). The primitive instantiated is
17	  % SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence, defined at
18	  % Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean:305
19	  % (section Equivalence, lines 289--329): "equivalence of SheafOfModules
20	  % categories from a site equivalence e : C \simeq D together with a morphism of
21	  % the two structure ring sheaves and its inverse, with two coherences". The
22	  % underlying site equivalence is TopologicalSpace.Opens.overEquivalence
23	  % (Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean:41), and the continuity prerequisites are
24	  % the documented TODO at Over.lean:19--22.
25	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subset, with associated open
26	  immersion \(\iota : \widetilde{U} \hookrightarrow X\). The reindexing site
27	  equivalence
28	  \[
29	    e \;:=\; \mathtt{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U
30	       \;:\; \mathtt{Over}\,U \;\simeq\; \mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}
31	  \]
32	  --- between the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\) (carrying the slice
33	  topology \((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\)) and the
34	  opens of the subspace \(\widetilde{U}\) (carrying
35	  \(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,\widetilde{U}\)) --- lifts to an
36	  \emph{equivalence of sheaf-of-modules categories}
37	  \[
38	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}\;:\;
39	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,\bigl((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
40	        \;\;\simeq\;\;
41	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr),
42	  \]
43	  the modules-level lift of \(e\) that transports the structure ring sheaf. It is
44	  obtained by instantiating
45	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) at \(e\), with the
46	  open-immersion structure-sheaf ring morphism
47	  \[
48	    \varphi \;:\;
49	      X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U
50	      \;\longrightarrow\;
51	      \bigl(e.\mathtt{functor}.\mathtt{sheafPushforwardContinuous}\bigr).\mathrm{obj}\,
52	        \bigl((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
53	  \]
54	  --- sectionwise at an object \(V \in \mathtt{Over}\,U\) the open-immersion
55	  comparison \(\mathcal{O}_X(V.\mathrm{left}) \to \mathcal{O}_{\widetilde{U}}(e\,V)\),
56	  i.e.\ the section-level isomorphism \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\) of the structure
57	  sheaves --- together with its inverse \(\psi\) and the two coherences
58	  \(H_1, H_2\) expressing that \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are mutually inverse.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	\begin{proof}
62	
63	\leanok
64	  The construction is the assembly of three existing Mathlib primitives; the only
65	  step carrying mathematical content is the ring morphism \(\varphi\).
66	
67	  \emph{Continuity (no work).} The equivalence
68	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) requires both legs of the site
69	  equivalence \(e\) to be continuous:
70	  \(e.\mathtt{functor}\) and \(e.\mathtt{inverse}\). For a dense-subsite inclusion
71	  continuity is automatic --- it is a typeclass instance --- and for an
72	  equivalence the density of one leg propagates to the other. The declaration
73	  \(\mathtt{overEquivInverseIsDenseSubsite}\) exhibits \(e.\mathtt{inverse}\) as a
74	  dense subsite of the slice site, from which both continuity legs follow by
75	  inference. The Mathlib TODO at \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22)
76	  is discharged for this case.
77	
78	  \emph{The ring morphism \(\varphi\) (the genuine content).} The two structure
79	  ring sheaves are different objects on different sites:
80	  \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\), and
81	  \((\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\).
82	  The open immersion \(\iota\) identifies them: at an object \(V \in
83	  \mathtt{Over}\,U\) the sections \(\mathcal{O}_X(V.\mathrm{left})\) and
84	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\widetilde{U}}(\iota^{-1}(V.\mathrm{left}))\) agree through the
85	  open-immersion section isomorphism \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\). This is verbatim the
86	  datum that \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor}\) already uses inline
87	  (its structure map is \((\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\,\cdot).\mathrm{inv}\)). Packaging
88	  this section-level isomorphism naturally in \(V\) yields \(\varphi\); its inverse
89	  is \(\psi\), and the coherences \(H_1, H_2\) record that the
90	  \(\iota.\mathtt{appIso}\) round-trips are identities, proved by the
91	  inverse-iso laws (\(\mathtt{Sheaf.hom\_ext}\) reduces them sectionwise).
92	
93	  Feeding \(e\), \(\varphi\), \(\psi\), \(H_1\), \(H_2\) into
94	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\) produces the asserted equivalence.
95	  Its underlying functor is \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\), which
96	  is what the two consumer isomorphisms below unfold.
97	\end{proof}

99	\section{The equivalence carries restriction and unit}
100	\label{sec:soe_consumers}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}
103	
104	\leanok
105	[The over-equivalence carries restriction to over]
106	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_restrict_over_iso}
107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictOverIso}
108	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
109	  Let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Under the over-equivalence of
110	  \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} there is a canonical isomorphism
111	  \[
112	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,\bigl(M|_\iota\bigr)
113	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
114	    M.\mathtt{over}\,U,
115	  \]
116	  i.e.\ the equivalence carries the restriction \(M|_\iota\) of \(M\) along the
117	  open immersion to the slice object \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U\).
118	\end{lemma}
119	
120	\begin{proof}
121	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
122	  \leanok
123	  The restriction \(M|_\iota\) is \emph{itself} a pushforward of sheaves of
124	  modules: by construction \(M|_\iota = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\)
125	  applied along \(\iota.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) (the open-immersion functor
126	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U} \to \mathtt{Opens}\,X\)). The functor underlying
127	  the over-equivalence is \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\). Their
128	  composite is again a single pushforward, identified by
129	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardComp}\), which here is the identity
130	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{Iso.refl}\). It remains to identify the two underlying
131	  index functors \(\mathtt{Over}\,U \to \mathtt{Opens}\,X\) entering the two
132	  pushforwards: both send an object \(V\) to its left component \(V.\mathrm{left}\),
133	  so they are equal, and \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatIso}\) along the \(\mathtt{eqToIso}\)
134	  of that equality transports the composite to \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U\). The whole
135	  argument mirrors, step for step, the existing
136	  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorAdjCounitIso}\).
137	\end{proof}
138	
139	\begin{lemma}
140	
141	\leanok
142	[The over-equivalence carries unit to unit]
143	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_unit_over_iso}
144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitOverIso}
145	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
146	  Under the over-equivalence of \cref{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence} there is
147	  a canonical isomorphism
148	  \[
149	    \mathtt{overEquivalence}.\mathtt{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,
150	      \bigl(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
151	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
152	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr),
153	  \]
154	  i.e.\ the equivalence carries the unit module (the structure sheaf-as-module) of
155	  the subspace site to the unit module of the slice site.
156	\end{lemma}
157	
158	\begin{proof}
159	  \uses{def:sheafofmodules_over_equivalence}
160	  \leanok
161	  The functor underlying the over-equivalence is
162	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,\varphi\). The pushforward of a unit module
163	  along a morphism of ringed sites is the unit module of the target ring sheaf,
164	  transported along the ring identification \(\varphi\): the structure sheaf
165	  pushes forward to the structure sheaf because \(\varphi\) is precisely the
166	  open-immersion identification of the two structure ring sheaves. Concretely the
167	  pushforward-of-unit computation produces \(\mathtt{unit}\) of the codomain ring
168	  sheaf up to \(\varphi\), and \(\varphi\) being an isomorphism makes this a
169	  genuine isomorphism.
170	
171	  Concretely, the canonical comparison map realising this transport is the
172	  unit-to-pushforward-of-unit morphism
173	  \[
174	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi
175	      \;:\;
176	      \mathtt{unit}\,\bigl(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}.\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr)
177	      \;\longrightarrow\;
178	      (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,
179	        \bigl(\mathtt{unit}\,(\widetilde{U}).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr).
180	  \]
181	  Its section at an open \(W\) is exactly the section-level ring map
182	  \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\): the morphism acts on a section by multiplication
183	  through \(\varphi\), so on \(W\) it is the additive-group map underlying
184	  \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\) (the characterisation
185	  \(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit\_val\_app\_apply}\)). Since \(\varphi\) is an
186	  isomorphism of ring sheaves, each component \(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,W\) is a ring
187	  isomorphism, hence its underlying additive-group map is an isomorphism.
188	
189	  It follows that \(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
190	  One reflects the sectionwise isomorphism upward through the two forgetful
191	  functors --- first \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.forget}\) (down to presheaves of
192	  modules), then \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf}\) (down to presheaves of
193	  additive groups) --- so that being an isomorphism reduces to the componentwise
194	  isomorphism check
195	  \(\mathtt{NatTrans.isIso\_iff\_isIso\_app}\) on the underlying natural
196	  transformation, which is the sectionwise statement just established. The desired
197	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{unitOverIso}\) is then the inverse of the resulting
198	  isomorphism, \((\mathtt{asIso}\,(\mathtt{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi))^{-1}\).
199	  This is the slice-site analogue of
200	  \(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnitIso}\).
201	\end{proof}

301	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
302	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
303	
304	\begin{definition}
305	
306	
307	\leanok
308	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
309	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
311	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
312	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
313	  \[
314	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
315	  \]
316	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
317	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
318	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
319	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
320	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
321	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
322	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
323	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
324	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
325	  canonical compatibility
326	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
327	     \;\cong\;
328	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
329	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
330	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
331	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
332	
333	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
334	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
335	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
336	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
337	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
338	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
339	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
340	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\begin{lemma}
344	
345	
346	\leanok
347	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
348	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
350	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
351	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
352	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
353	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
354	  \[
355	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
357	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
358	  \]
359	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
360	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
361	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
362	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
363	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
364	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
365	  \[
366	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
367	    \qquad
368	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
369	  \]
370	  the associator
371	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
372	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
373	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
374	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
375	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
376	\end{lemma}
377	
378	\begin{proof}
379	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
380	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
381	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
382	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
383	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
384	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
385	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
386	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module

13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
16	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
17	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
18	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
19	derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
20	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
21	available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
22	over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
23	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
24	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
25	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
26	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
27	
28	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
29	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
30	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
31	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
32	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
33	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
34	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
35	\[
36	  \begin{array}{ccc}
37	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
38	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
39	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
40	  \end{array}
41	\]
42	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
43	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
44	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).

19	% (the converse spreading-out, Stacks Lemma~0B8M when stalks are local) is

22	
23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:lbc_setup}
25	
26	Fix a scheme \(X\) and write \(X.\mathtt{Modules}\) for the category of sheaves
27	of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) on the
28	ringed-space site of \(X\), with structure sheaf \(X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)).
29	A \emph{line bundle} on \(X\) is an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module; in the
30	sense of \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} we model line bundles by the
31	predicate \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}\): a module \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) is locally
32	trivial of rank one if every point \(x \in X\) has an \emph{affine} open
33	neighbourhood \(U\) on which the restriction \(M|_U\) is isomorphic, as an
34	\(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, to the structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U}\):
35	\[
36	  \texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M) \;:=\;
37	  \forall x \in X,\ \exists\, U \ni x \text{ affine open},\quad
38	  M|_U \;\cong\; \struct{U}.
39	\]
40	This is the rank-one case of Stacks Definition~17.25.1
41	(Tag~01CS, invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules), and
42	agrees with the classical ``locally free of rank one'' description by Stacks
43	Lemma~17.25.4 (Tag~0B8M).
44	
45	The goal of the chapter is to record that such a module is \emph{coherent} in the
46	sense the Quot construction consumes, namely finitely presented:
47	\(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) in Mathlib's
48	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\) (built from a
49	\(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\)). The mathematics is elementary: on each
50	trivialising chart \(M|_U \cong \struct{U}\) is finite free of rank one, hence
51	has the trivial finite presentation, and finite presentation is by definition a
52	local condition. No stalk computation and no spreading-out are involved. The
53	content is the bookkeeping that turns the pointwise trivialisation into the
54	indexed \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) Mathlib expects.
55

57	\label{sec:lbc_site_instances}
58	
59	% NOTE (chief unknown). Mathlib's SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation /

111	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
112	  %  {\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
113	  %  of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
114	  %  the functor
115	  %  $$

122	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CS (Modules, Definition~17.25.1).}
123	  Let \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\).
124	  Then there exist an index set \(I\), a family of affine open subschemes
125	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(X\) covering \(X\) (i.e. \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\)), and
126	  for each \(i\) an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}

45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf

62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
